Spatial Multi-Omics Dissection of Subsister Niches: 
Predicting and Overcoming Chemotherapy Resistance in Ovarian Cancer

Sara Palomino-Echeverria1,2,3,4, Ada Junquera1, Matías M. Falco1, Tatiana Belova3, Ceren Pajanoja4, Jun Dai1, Hanna Elomaa1, Wenqing Chen1 , Ping-an Hsieh5, Anna Vähärautio1,6, Mariike L. Kuijjer3,4,8*, Anniina Färkkilä1,6,7*

1Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland; 2Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, HiLIFE, Helsinki, Finland; 3Computational Biology and Systems Medicine, Norwegian Centre for Molecular Biosciences and Medicine, University of Oslo, Oslo, Norway; 4Department of Biochemistry and Developmental Biology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland; 5Department of Biomechatronics Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan; 6Foundation for the Finnish Cancer Institute, Helsinki, Finland; 7Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland ; 8iCAN- Digital Precision Cancer Medicine, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

High-Grade Serous Carcinoma remains the most lethal gynecologic malignancy due to relapse (1). The driver of this clinical failure is not a single genetic mutation, but a resilient, diverse population of cells that survive initial therapy (2).

The "Subsister" Framework

1). The driver of this clinical failure is not a single genetic mutation, but a resilient, diverse population of cells that survive initial therapy (2).

The "Subsister" Framework
We define Subsister Niches as the functional unit of recurrence. Unlike traditional persisters, subsisters are not just individual cells, but spatially organized survival units integrated within the Tumor Microenvironment (TME).

To map the spatial transcriptomic and proteomic landscape of these niches, identifying the specific TME interactions that protect subsister niches from cytotoxic pressure and drive post-therapy relapse

Paired scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics reveal six distinct functional tumor phenotypes within the microenvironment.

Cross-modality validation confirms concordant mRNA and protein expression patterns.

Figure 4: Multi-modal validation of tumor-metaclusters signatures. Comparison of mRNA signatures detected via Xenium (up) and corresponding protein expression via t-CycIF (down). The high degree of spatial overlap between the transcriptomic and proteomic signals validates the accuracy of the functional phenotypes identified in Figure 3.

ia t-CycIF (down). The high degree of spatial overlap between the transcriptomic and proteomic signals validates the accuracy of the functional phenotypes identified in Figure 3.

Figure 3: Integrated single-cell and spatial landscape of HGSC. (A) UMAP projection of the paired dataset (left) with sub-clustering of the tumor cell compartment (right), identifying six functional metaclusters. (B) Spatial validation of tumor phenotypes in Xenium. Representative spatial niches (top) correspond to the marker gene expression profiles shown in the dot plot (bottom), confirming the distinct transcriptomic signatures of each functional phenotype.

Figure 2: Study Design and Multi-Omic Integration Pipeline. (A) Clinical cohort of 16 HGSC patients, Primary Debulking Surgery (PDS) vs. Interval Debulking Surgery (IDS).(B-C) Parallel processing of fresh and FFPE tissue for single-cell (scRNA-seq) and spatial (Xenium + t-CycIF) analysis.(D) Computational framework for hierarchical data integration, identifying common cell identities and distinct functional states using CAVACHON (3). (E) Validation through bulk RNA-seq and derived gene signature.

Figure 1: Conceptual framework of the HGSC subsister niche. Therapeutic pressure selects for resilient cancer cells within immuno-suppressive niches, driving clonal selection and resistant recurrence.

INTRODUCTION

CONCLUSIONS

ster niche. Therapeutic pressure selects for resilient cancer cells within immuno-suppressive niches, driving clonal selection and resistant recurrence.

INTRODUCTION

CONCLUSIONS
Diverse Phenotypes: Identified six distinct functional tumor states with specific transcriptomic signatures.
Spatial Concordance: Confirmed high correlation between mRNA and protein signals across modalities.
Subsister Niches: Functional states organize into non-random spatial niches, representing specialized survival microenvironments.


FUTURE LINE
CAVACHON Integration: Use hierarchical modeling to quantify RNA vs. protein contributions to each functional state, incorporating morphology.
Predictive Biomarker Development: Use integrated spatial signatures to develop models predicting recurrence in HGSC patients.

CONTACT

Quantifying the relative contribution of RNA and protein modalities to the observed tumor phenotypes.

B.

A.

Figure 5: CAVACHON framework for multi-modal contribution analysis. (A) Hierarchical graphical model integrating Xenium (RNA) and t-CycIF (protein) data. (B) Computational workflow for deriving modality contribution scores. By perturbing the latent space (ZRNA​, ZProtein​), we quantify how much each data type drives the observed differential gene expression, enabling the identification of modality-specific functional drivers.

Leveraging morphological and spatial features to refine the latent representation of tumor phenotypes

Figure 6: Multi-modal refinement using morphology and spatial context. Non-transcriptomic features (cell shape, size, and neighborhood proximity) are integrated into the pipeline to refine the latent space. This approach aims to resolve functionally distinct cell states that appear similar at the transcriptomic level but differ in their physical architecture and spatial organization.
